verses.text: ‘Oh that thou wouldest rend the heavens, that thou wouldest come down.’ The prophet pleads for a divine theophany. The Hebrew qara — to tear — is violent: rip open the sky. Mark 1:10 will echo this language at Jesus’ baptism when the heavens are torn open. What Isaiah longed for, the gospel will record as actually happening.

Isaiah imagines what such a coming would look like: a fire so hot it boils water, mountains shaken at God’s presence, nations trembling. The prophet wants the world to know who God is. There is something fierce about the longing for revelation — the desire that hidden truths become visible enough to silence the loud objections of unbelief.

‘When thou didst terrible things which we looked not for.’ The Hebrew nora’ot — awesome, terrifying acts — recalls past theophanies like Sinai. Israel knows God can act in stunning ways; the prophet pleads for Him to act again. Memory of past grace fuels prayer for present help.

‘Since the beginning of the world men have not heard, nor perceived by the ear, neither hath the eye seen, O God, beside thee, what he hath prepared for him that waiteth for him.’ Paul will quote this verse in 1 Corinthians 2:9. The waiting God prepares things for waiters. The reward of patient hope is not a thing the eye has ever seen; it is what God Himself has prepared.
64:5-6 constant sinners . . . infected and impure with sin: See also 59:1-15. The only hope for salvation and transformation is through Jesus Christ (see Rom 7:25–8:11).
64:5-6 constant sinners . . . infected and impure with sin: See also 59:1-15. The only hope for salvation and transformation is through Jesus Christ (see Rom 7:25–8:11).
64:7 you have turned away from us: See 1:15; 30:20; 59:2. • turned us over to our sins: Because the people were so bent on sinning, God left them to perish in their sinful ways (see Rom 1:24).
64:10 holy cities: All the cities of Judah were considered holy to God. However, the Temple in the host city of Jerusalem was the most holy place.
